column2: What are the three formal requirements for consensus?
What does the FLP impossibility result state?
column3: No deterministic algorithm can guarantee consensus in an asynchronous system with even one possible failure
column4: Consensus is impossible in synchronous systems
column5: Byzantine faults make consensus impossible in all settings
column6: Consensus requires at least 3 nodes in any system
column7: No randomized algorithm can achieve consensus

column2: How many nodes are required to tolerate f crash failures in Raft/Paxos (majority-quorum approach)?
How many nodes are required to tolerate f Byzantine (traitor) failures?
In Paxos Phase 1 (Prepare/Promise), what does an acceptor promise when it receives a Prepare(N) message?
column3: Not to accept any proposal numbered less than N, and to return the highest-numbered accepted proposal
column4: To immediately accept value N as the chosen value
column5: To forward the Prepare message to all other acceptors
column6: To reject all future Prepare messages with number > N
column7: To send its own proposal to the proposer

column2: In Raft, what condition must a candidate satisfy to win a leader election?
column3: Its log must be at least as up-to-date as the log of a majority of nodes
column4: It must have the highest node ID in the cluster
column5: It must have the longest log in the cluster
column6: It must receive votes from all nodes in the cluster
column7: It must have received the last heartbeat from the old leader

column2: In Raft, when does a leader consider a log entry from the CURRENT term committed?
column3: When a majority of servers have acknowledged storing the entry
column4: When the entry has been applied to the leader's own state machine
column5: When any single follower has acknowledged the entry
column6: When the entry has been stored on all servers
column7: When the term number of the entry exceeds the previous committed entry's term

column2: Which real-world system uses Raft as its consensus algorithm?
Why does Raft NOT allow committing log entries from previous terms directly, even if stored on a majority?
column3: A later leader with a conflicting entry could still win an election and overwrite those entries
column4: Entries from previous terms are always uncommitted and must be discarded
column5: Entries can only be committed by the leader who originally created them
column6: The term number stored in the entry would overflow if committed across terms
column7: Previous-term entries are only stored locally and never replicated

column2: What is the key practical trade-off that Raft makes when a network partition occurs?
column3: Raft prioritises consistency (safety) over availability: the minority partition stops making progress
column4: Raft prioritises availability: both partitions continue to serve clients independently
column5: Raft uses a randomised algorithm so both partitions may decide differently
column6: Raft waits indefinitely until the partition heals before making any decision
column7: Raft promotes a leader in each partition to maintain full availability
